criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the core EXPO methodology, including the first-order approximation formulation and the extrapolation formula (Equation 6).

The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the controlled experiment setup, including the training recipe, UltraFeedback dataset split (61K training, 1K development), and evaluation protocol on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The agent has read Table 1 to understand the motivation: parameter change from SFT to DPO (normalized Frobenius norm ~6.348e-6) is significantly smaller than from Pre-trained to SFT (~1.955e-4), justifying the small parameter change hypothesis.
The agent has read Section 3.2 and Table 2 to understand the varying training steps experiment (10%, 20%, 40%, 100% of 478 steps) and the EXPO results on AlpacaEval 2.0.
The agent has read Section 4.1 and Table 5 to understand the extended application of EXPO to 12 open-source LLMs trained via offline DPO, iterative DPO, and online RLHF.

The plan includes loading the SFT checkpoint (M0) and the alignment-trained model (M1) from HuggingFace, computing the parameter difference Δθ = θ₁ - θ₀ per tensor, and constructing the extrapolated model θ₂ = θ₁ + α·Δθ as specified in Equation 6.

The plan includes hyperparameter search for the optimal α using reward model scoring on the UltraFeedback development set (1K instructions), combining binary search and grid search with manually tuned intervals as described in Appendix B.

The plan includes the interpolation validation experiment varying γ within [0, 1] to construct interpolated models θ₀ + γΔθ and evaluating their alignment performance using reward models (RM-Mistral-7B and FsfairX-LLaMA3-RM-v0.1).

The plan includes the controlled experiment on varying training steps: training DPO models with 10%, 20%, 40%, and 100% of the full 478-step training, applying EXPO to each, and evaluating on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The plan includes DPO training configuration: UltraFeedback dataset, zephyr-7b-sft-full as initialization and reference model, global batch size 128, learning rate 5e-7, AdamW optimizer, trained on 8 A100 80GB GPUs.
The plan includes inference configuration using vLLM: top-k sampling (k=40), nucleus sampling (p=0.9), temperature=0.7, presence penalty=0.1, frequency penalty=0.1, and random seed=42.

The plan includes the extended application of EXPO to 12 existing open-source LLMs (zephyr-7b-alpha/beta, tulu2-7/13/70b, snorkel-7b-iter, llama3-8b-iter, starling-7b-alpha/beta, internlm2-1.8/7/20b) with grid search in [0.1, 0.5] at 0.1 intervals.

The core EXPO model extrapolation function is implemented: given M0 (SFT, parameterized by θ₀) and M1 (aligned model, parameterized by θ₁), compute Δθ = θ₁ - θ₀ for each parameter tensor and produce M2 with parameters θ₂ = θ₁ + α·Δθ = θ₀ + (1+α)·Δθ as per Equation 6.

The code implements the Frobenius norm computation (and a normalized variant) to analyze parameter changes between checkpoint pairs (Pre-trained vs SFT, SFT vs DPO, Pre-trained vs DPO) as shown in Table 1.
The code implements linear interpolation to construct models parameterized by θ₀ + γΔθ = (1-γ)θ₀ + γθ₁ for varying γ ∈ [0, 1], to validate the first-order approximation as shown in Figure 1.

The code loads and applies the two reward models (RM-Mistral-7B from 'weqweasdas/RM-Mistral-7B' and FsfairX-LLaMA3-RM-v0.1 from 'sfairXC/FsfairX-LLaMA3-RM-v0.1') to score model outputs on the UltraFeedback development set.
The code implements model inference using vLLM with the specified settings: top-k=40, nucleus sampling p=0.9, temperature=0.7, presence_penalty=0.1, frequency_penalty=0.1, seed=42.

The code implements DPO training using the UltraFeedback dataset (61K training samples), with the SFT checkpoint 'alignment-handbook/zephyr-7b-sft-full' as both initialization and reference model, global batch size 128, learning rate 5e-7, and AdamW optimizer.

The code supports training DPO models with varying proportions of the full training steps (10%, 20%, 40%, 100% of 478 steps total), allowing model checkpointing at each proportion for subsequent EXPO application.

The hyperparameter search procedure for α is implemented using a combination of binary search and grid search with dynamically adjusted intervals, selecting the α that maximizes expected reward on the UltraFeedback development set as scored by RM-Mistral-7B.

The length-biased training data experiment is implemented: training data is sorted by length difference between preferred and non-preferred responses in descending order, so models prioritize samples with larger length differences, as described in Section 3.3.

The training configuration ablation is implemented to train models with different epochs (1, 2, 3), learning rates (5e-7, 1e-6, 2e-6), and optimizers (AdamW, AdaGrad, RMSprop) as specified in Table 4.

The code loads the correct HuggingFace model pairs for each of the 12 extended models, using the SFT (M0) and aligned (M1) checkpoints specified in Appendix C (e.g., 'allenai/tulu-2-7b' as M0 and 'allenai/tulu-2-dpo-7b' as M1 for tulu2-7b).

The code applies EXPO to models trained via diverse alignment algorithms (RRHF, SLiC-HF, IPO, CPO, KTO, R-DPO, SimPO) using Mistral-7B and LLaMA-3-based SFT checkpoints from princeton-nlp, as described in Section 4.2 and Table 6.

The interpolation experiment (Figure 1) is executed successfully, evaluating alignment performance of interpolated models under two reward models for multiple DPO/RLHF model pairs as γ varies from 0 to 1.
The DPO training at varying training steps (10%, 20%, 40%, 100%) runs successfully, producing intermediate checkpoints that are then used for EXPO application and evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0.

The hyperparameter search for α is executed successfully for each partially-trained model (10%, 20%, 40%, 100% steps), producing the optimal α values (8.0, 2.5, 0.5, 0.3 respectively) consistent with Table 7.

The training data quality ablation (Table 3) is executed successfully for both random and length-biased sampling at 10%, 20%, and 40% training steps, including EXPO application and reward/length measurement.

The training configuration ablation (Table 4) is executed successfully for all combinations of training epochs (1, 2, 3), learning rates (5e-7, 1e-6, 2e-6), and optimizers (AdamW, AdaGrad, RMSprop) with EXPO applied.
EXPO is applied and evaluated on the 12 extended open-source LLMs on both AlpacaEval 2.0 (win rate and LC win rate) and MT-Bench, with optimal α searched in [0.1, 0.5] at 0.1 intervals.

EXPO is applied to models trained via different alignment algorithms (RRHF, SLiC-HF, IPO, CPO, KTO, R-DPO, SimPO) for both Mistral and LLaMA-3 base models, with results evaluated via reward model scoring on UltraFeedback.

The execution outcome matches Figure 1: interpolated models (γ ∈ [0,1]) consistently achieve intermediate alignment performance between the original SFT (γ=0) and DPO/RLHF (γ=1) models, and performance increases monotonically with γ for the evaluated models.

The execution outcome matches Table 2: EXPO boosts M1 with 20% training steps (LC win rate 12.9% → 21.3%) to outperform the fully-trained M1 (LC win rate 17.3%), and EXPO improves all partially-trained models.

The execution outcome matches Table 2: the EXPO-applied model trained with 20% steps (M2^{20%}) achieves a reward score of ~6.95, LC win rate ~21.3%, and win rate ~22.7%, outperforming the fully-trained M1 (reward ~6.16, LC WR ~17.3%).

The execution outcome matches Table 3: length-biased training data consistently undermines EXPO effectiveness, resulting in much smaller optimal α values and lower reward improvements compared to random sampling at the same training step proportion.

The execution outcome matches Table 4: AdamW (default, 1 epoch) yields the best EXPO gain (reward 4.37→6.08, α=2.5); increasing epochs to 3 or training with RMSprop eliminates EXPO benefit (N/A); AdaGrad achieves highest M2 reward despite slowest M1 convergence.

The execution outcome matches Table 5: EXPO consistently improves all 12 evaluated LLMs, with the largest improvements of up to 10.1% win rate (internlm2-20b), 4.5% LC win rate (tulu2-70b), and 0.37 on MT-Bench (llama3-8b-iter).

The execution outcome matches Table 6: EXPO improves models trained with most alignment algorithms (RRHF, SLiC-HF, IPO, CPO, R-DPO, SimPO) for both Mistral and LLaMA-3 bases, with KTO being the exception for the Mistral base (N/A in Table 6).
